product-no-team.title: You have a product in your head and no team to build it.
product-no-team.problem: You can see the thing clearly. You just have no developers, designers, or budget to assemble them.
product-no-team.approach: You describe what you want to exist and make the calls on what it should be. The interface, the logic, and the working thing get built with you.
product-no-team.ownership: You own all of it, yours to change.

business-unknown-field.title: You want to open a business in a field you don't know yet.
business-unknown-field.problem: You have the idea but you'd need a strategist, an analyst, and a row of consultants just to begin.
business-unknown-field.approach: You bring the idea. You get the market read, the model, the brand, the plan, and the first steps, in language you understand and shaped to your situation.
business-unknown-field.shape: A clear plan in days; the build that follows scoped with you.
business-unknown-field.ownership: Yours to decide and move on.

campaign-at-scale.title: You need a campaign across several platforms at once.
campaign-at-scale.problem: Different formats, different languages, and one of you. Stitching it from five tools makes it incoherent.
campaign-at-scale.approach: You set the intent and the message. You get the concept, the assets, and the variations across formats and languages, coherent because it was orchestrated as one.
campaign-at-scale.shape: A full campaign in a fraction of the usual time.

manual-process.title: Your team is drowning in a manual process.
manual-process.problem: The work is repetitive and slow, and every fix needs a vendor or a new hire.
manual-process.approach: You describe the workflow and where it hurts. You get it mapped, redesigned, and automated, with you deciding what stays in human hands.

raise-story-numbers.title: You're raising and need the story and the numbers.
raise-story-numbers.problem: You need a model, a thesis, and a deck that hold up under scrutiny, fast.
raise-story-numbers.approach: You bring the business. You get the model, the thesis, and the deck, built to hold up, in your voice, with you directing what it argues.

internal-tool.title: You want a tool your team uses every day.
internal-tool.problem: A CRM or internal system shaped to how you actually work, not a generic product you bend yourself around.
internal-tool.approach: You describe how your team works. You get a system built to fit.

new-market.title: You're entering a new market.
new-market.problem: You need research, localization, and a go-to-market built for that market specifically.
new-market.approach: You bring the ambition. You get the research, the localization, and the go-to-market, built for that market, with you steering.
new-market.shape: A real plan and the assets to start, quickly.

idea-carried-for-years.title: You've carried an idea for years and never built it.

I've paid for builds before and ended up with something I don't control.

I can't afford the team a real venture needs.
I want to own what I build, not depend on a vendor.
entrepreneurs.change: The market read, the model, the brand, the build — directed by you, owned by you, fast.

operations-leaders.name: I run operations
operations-leaders.headline: Run leaner and move faster, without headcount for every initiative.
operations-leaders.lead: Execute the initiatives that used to require a new team, with control and efficiency.
problems: Every new initiative means new cost and headcount.
We depend on vendors for everything.
Our processes are manual and leaky.
operations-leaders.change: Map, redesign, and automate the work — you decide what stays human, and you own the result.

business-owners.name: I lead a company
business-owners.headline: Solve the problem without assembling a cast of experts first.
business-owners.lead: Carrying a business problem, often outside your expertise? Understand it and solve it, with you in control.
problems: I'm facing a problem in an area I don't know.
Hiring consultants is slow and expensive.
I want clarity and a path, not a 90-page deck.
business-owners.change: The analysis, the options, and a clear recommendation you can act on — directed by you.

domain-newcomers.name: I'm entering something I don't understand yet
domain-newcomers.headline: Act without first becoming an expert, or hiring a row of them.
domain-newcomers.lead: "I'd need a CFO, a strategist, a designer" — the dependency itself is the block. It doesn't have to be.
problems: I'd need to hire a row of specialists just to start.
I'm blocked by what I don't know.
I want to move without becoming an expert first.
domain-newcomers.change: Expertise on demand, with you in control — you get what you'd hire for, shaped to your situation.

creatives.name: I create
creatives.headline: Produce far beyond what your headcount should allow.
creatives.lead: Leverage inside your own craft — the scale and quality that normally needs a full production apparatus.
problems: I'm capped by resources and budget, not ideas.
I can't produce at the scale I see in my head.
Stitching tools breaks the coherence.
creatives.change: Concept, production, and variations made as one coherent body of work — yours to run.

marketing-leaders.name: I run marketing
marketing-leaders.headline: The growth capability you direct yourself.
marketing-leaders.lead: Campaigns, content, and growth without the cost and slowness of an agency or a large internal team.
problems: An agency is slow and expensive.
An internal team is a big commitment.
I want output and speed I control.
marketing-leaders.change: Campaigns and content across channels, orchestrated as one, fast, and in your control.

enterprises.name: We're an enterprise
enterprises.headline: Serious capability you own, with your data kept close.
enterprises.lead: Capability without vendor lock-in, with data in the region and handled to local standards. Infrastructure you build on.
problems: Vendor lock-in and data exposure are real risks.
We want capability we own and control.
We need it built for how we actually work.
enterprises.change: Custom systems and capability, owned by you, with data kept in the region — not a vendor you're tied to.
